problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem D ("Mining the Influence of Music"). Teams were given an artist influence dataset (influencer->follower edges), per-artist/per-work audio feature data (danceability, valence, acousticness, energy, tempo, etc.), and popularity data, and asked to: (1) build a directed musical-influence network and quantify each artist's influence; (2) define a music-similarity metric and compare within- vs between-genre similarity; (3) characterize genres and how they evolve over time; (4) verify whether influencers actually shape followers' music and find which features are most "contagious"; (5) detect periods of musical revolution and the artists who drive them; (6) identify dynamic genre leaders; (7) discuss the social/external drivers of musical change. Note: the corpus metadata labels this "Problem D / operations_research," but the actual paper is the network/data-science music problem, not a classic OR optimization problem.
models_used: Directed influence network G={V,E}: each vertex = artist (id, main genre, active-start time), directed edge influencer->follower

Five network influence indicators: (1) Betweenness Centrality (normalized), (2) Eigenvector Centrality (largest-eigenvalue formulation Ax=λx), (3) Three Degree of Influence (TDI) = size of BFS subnetwork to depth 3, (4) Follower Loyalty FL = sum over followers of 1/In-Degree (loyalty split evenly among a follower's influencers), (5) Diversity of Influenced Genres (DIG) = count of distinct genres within 3 generations
Entropy Weight Method (EWM) to combine the 5 indicators into a single Music Influence (MI) score MI(v)=Σ w_i·indicator_i; per-follower influence MI(v)_t = MI(v)·Loy(t)
Breadth-First Search (BFS) to depth 3 to build per-artist subnetworks (used for both TDI and DIG)
Factor Analysis (scikit-learn FactorAnalysis, X=HW+M+E) to reduce ~12 audio features to a 3-D latent space (acoustics-energy, valence, instrumental components)
Pearson correlation matrix of audio features to motivate the dimensionality reduction
Cosine-similarity-based genre similarity matrix S_ij = average over artist pairs of cos(x,y) within/between genres
Genre fingerprinting via three methods: low-variance feature detection, latent-space cluster analysis, and outlier detection using violin plots
Hypothesis testing for influencer->follower effect: Jarque-Bera normality test to choose Pearson vs Spearman, then Spearman correlation comparing connected pairs (Group 1) vs random pairs (Group 2)

Bollinger Bands + Moving Average (window=3yr, std factor β=2) on the 3 latent component time series to detect music revolutions (signal = MA breaks the band with high slope); justified via Pauta/3σ criterion

First-Order Temporal Correlation Coefficient (CORT) between an artist's feature trajectory and the industry-average trajectory to identify revolution leaders (Algorithm 1, with influence threshold c1 and correlation threshold c2)
Dynamic Influencer Evaluation Index (DIEI): time-varying EWM combination of MI score, average popularity, and total popularity to find per-genre, per-year leaders
key_techniques: Reusing one artifact across many sub-problems: the depth-3 BFS subnetwork feeds TDI, DIG, and the subnetwork analysis; the 3 Factor-Analysis components feed similarity, genre evolution, revolution detection, and leader detection. This gives the whole paper a single coherent spine.

Entropy Weight Method as the recurring 'objective weighting' device — used both for the static MI score and again (time-varying) for the DIEI, framed explicitly as 'let the data speak, no human-set weights.'

Motivating dimensionality reduction with evidence: they first show a feature correlation matrix proving features are correlated, so Euclidean distance in raw space is invalid, THEN introduce Factor Analysis as the fix — a clean problem->justification->method chain.

Borrowing a finance tool (Bollinger Bands) and reframing it as a statistical confidence band (linked to the 3σ Pauta criterion) to turn 'detect a revolution' into a concrete, testable rule: MA crosses the band with high slope.

Operationalizing fuzzy words: 'loyalty' = 1/in-degree, 'revolution leader' = high MI + high temporal sync (CORT≈1), 'contagious feature' = larger Spearman gap between connected vs random pairs. Each vague concept gets a formula.
Choosing the right correlation test by first testing the assumption: Jarque-Bera normality check decides Pearson vs Spearman rather than blindly assuming normality.

Sensitivity analysis targeted at the actual hyper-parameters that matter (MA window 3/5/9 yr; thresholds c1, c2) and showing the qualitative conclusion (1961-1968 revolution; Beatles/Nat King Cole) is stable across them.
why_it_won: From a judge's view, this paper wins on coherence and decisiveness rather than on any single heavy model. It (1) covers all seven sub-questions with a unified two-space framework (network space + latent feature space) so nothing feels bolted on; (2) gives every claim a concrete operational definition and a formula, so the work is reproducible and falsifiable; (3) backs methodological choices with evidence (correlation matrix before FA, normality test before correlation, 3σ rationale before Bollinger Bands) instead of asserting them; (4) produces memorable, checkable headline findings (1961-1968 revolution, Beatles and Nat King Cole as pioneers, acousticness/valence as the most contagious features) that a judge can sanity-check against real music history and find plausible; (5) includes genuine sensitivity analysis showing the conclusions survive parameter changes; and (6) is honest about its weaknesses (limited data, abandoned indirect-influence REL function). The strong visual layer — latent-space scatter, genre similarity heatmap, Sankey of inter-genre influence, violin plots, banded time series — lets a judge grasp each result in seconds.
section_highlights: The Summary + "Our Work" bullets give a one-line answer to each of the 7 problems up front, so a judge knows the whole story before page 5. The mermaid workflow diagram (Figure 1) maps the entire pipeline visually. Problem 1 is the strongest section: 5 clearly-defined, separately-motivated indicators each with a formula, combined by EWM with the weight table (EC highest at 0.316, FL lowest at 0.115) and a concrete MI scoreboard (Beatles 0.587, Miles Davis 0.520). Problem 2 nails the justify-then-reduce pattern (correlation heatmap -> Factor Analysis -> latent scatter showing genre clusters -> cosine similarity heatmap with the "diagonal is largest" within-genre observation). Problem 5 is the show-stopper: Bollinger-Band revolution detection pinned to 1961-1968 plus the CORT-based Algorithm 1 (clean pseudocode) yielding Beatles and Nat King Cole. The Sensitivity Analysis section (MA window 3/5/9; thresholds c1,c2) and an unusually candid Weaknesses section (abandoned REL indirect-influence function, failed 3-generation extension) signal mature, honest modeling. The "Document to the ICM Society" closes by naming concrete future upgrades (LSTM/VAE for time series, GNN for the network).
pitfalls_or_limitations: The hypothesis test is logically inverted: H0 is stated as 'followers ARE influenced' (the thing they want to be true), and they 'accept H0' — proper practice puts the claim as the alternative and rejects a null of no-effect. The Spearman comparison is suggestive but the formal test framing is wrong.

Inconsistent reporting of which features are most 'contagious': the Summary says 'acousticness and valence,' Section 1.2 says 'acousticness and energy.' This kind of internal contradiction is a credibility risk.

Genre/work-count and feature-trend conclusions rest on figures whose underlying numbers (as transcribed here) look schematic/illustrative rather than precisely from data; some charts appear stylized, weakening the data-driven claim.

Causation is asserted from correlation: connected pairs being more similar than random pairs does not establish that the influencer caused the follower's style (shared era, shared genre, homophily are unaddressed confounders).

Eigenvector centrality on a directed influence graph can be numerically fragile (sources/sinks, non-strongly-connected components); the paper doesn't discuss convergence or which adjacency direction was used.

Bollinger Bands assume an approximately stationary/normal process over a rolling window; applying a 3σ confidence-band rationale to a strongly trending latent component is a stretch, and a single global revolution window (1961-1968) is identified somewhat by eye.

Indirect influence was dropped: the REL (harmonic-mean shortest-path) function and the 3-generation extension both 'gave unsatisfying results' and were abandoned, so Problem 4 only uses direct edges — a real limitation they acknowledge but don't resolve.
Problem 7 (sociality) is largely qualitative narrative (counterculture, Internet) with little quantitative linkage back to the data.
reusable_patterns: Single-spine architecture: build two canonical representations early (a network + a low-dim latent feature space) and route EVERY sub-question through them, so the paper reads as one model, not seven disconnected answers.

Composite scoring template: define several orthogonal, separately-justified indicators, then fuse them with Entropy Weight Method into one interpretable score (MI). Reuse the same EWM machinery with time-varying weights for a second score (DIEI).
Justify-before-reduce: show a feature correlation matrix to prove redundancy, THEN apply Factor Analysis/PCA — turns dimensionality reduction from an arbitrary choice into a necessity.
Operationalize every fuzzy noun: pick a one-line formula for each vague term in the prompt ('loyalty'=1/in-degree, 'revolution'=MA breaks Bollinger Band, 'leader'=CORT≈1). Judges reward concreteness.

Borrow a domain tool and re-derive it statistically: importing Bollinger Bands (finance) for change-point detection and tying it to the 3σ Pauta criterion is a clean, defensible move for any 'detect a sudden shift over time' sub-problem.
Assumption-checked statistics: run a normality test (Jarque-Bera) to choose Pearson vs Spearman rather than defaulting — cheap rigor that signals statistical maturity.

Targeted sensitivity analysis: vary only the true hyper-parameters (smoothing window, decision thresholds) and demonstrate that the headline conclusion is invariant — far more convincing than perturbing everything.
Memorable, checkable headline results: produce named, real-world-verifiable findings (specific era, specific artists, specific features) that a judge can sanity-check against domain knowledge.
Honest, specific weaknesses section: naming an abandoned method (REL indirect-influence) and a failed extension (3-generation) builds trust far more than a generic 'our model could use more data.'

Visual vocabulary kit for network+feature problems: latent-space scatter for clusters, cosine-similarity heatmap for within/between comparisons, Sankey/chord for directional flow between groups, violin plots for outlier-based class fingerprinting, banded time series for change detection.
